assertion_results.text: Data extraction correctly identifies CVE-2026-31812, library quinn-proto, affected range < 0.11.14, and fixed version 0.11.14 from the Vulnerability issue (Step 1)
assertion_results.evidence: data-extraction.md Step 1 table contains: CVE ID = CVE-2026-31812, Vulnerable library = quinn-proto, Affected version range = 'versions before 0.11.14 (< 0.11.14)', Fixed version = 0.11.14. All four fields correctly extracted.

assertion_results.text: Data extraction identifies the ecosystem as Cargo based on the library name and component context (Step 1 Ecosystem detection)
assertion_results.evidence: data-extraction.md lines 59-63: 'The vulnerable library quinn-proto is a Rust crate. The ecosystem is Cargo, which is configured in the Ecosystem Mappings table for both streams: Lock file: Cargo.lock, Check command: git show <tag>:Cargo.lock'. Ecosystem identified as Cargo based on the library being a Rust crate.

assertion_results.text: Version impact table includes ALL versions from the security-matrix.md supportability matrix — no versions are skipped (Important Rule 4)
assertion_results.evidence: version-impact.md table includes 7 versions across both streams: 2.1.0 (v0.3.8), 2.1.1 (v0.3.12), 2.2.0 (v0.4.5), 2.2.1 (v0.4.8), 2.2.2 (v0.4.9), 2.2.3 (v0.4.11), 2.2.4 (v0.4.12). These cover both the 2.1.x stream (2 versions) and 2.2.x stream (5 versions). The affects-versions.md and remediation.md reference these same versions consistently, confirming no versions are missing.

assertion_results.text: Version impact analysis uses pinned commit tags from the supportability matrix for each version, not branch HEAD (Important Rule 13)
assertion_results.evidence: version-impact.md table shows specific pinned Build Tags for each version: v0.3.8, v0.3.12, v0.4.5, v0.4.8, v0.4.9, v0.4.11, v0.4.12. data-extraction.md line 61 confirms the check command is 'git show <tag>:Cargo.lock' using tags, not branch HEAD. The quinn-proto versions found at each tag differ (0.11.9, 0.11.12, 0.11.14), confirming tag-specific lookups were used.

assertion_results.text: Version impact table identifies retag versions and carries forward the affected status from the original version (Important Rule 5)
assertion_results.evidence: version-impact.md line 11: '2.2.2 | 2.2.x | v0.4.9 | -- | YES | retag of 2.2.1 (same as v0.4.8)'. The quinn-proto column shows '--' (no independent lookup), and the Notes column explicitly states 'retag of 2.2.1'. The Analysis section lines 20-21 further explains: 'Version 2.2.2 is a retag of 2.2.1 (build tag v0.4.9 reuses v0.4.8 backend sources), so the lock file check was skipped and the result carried forward from 2.2.1.'

assertion_results.text: Affects Versions correction proposes replacing PSIRT-assigned versions with lock-file-verified versions, showing the diff (Step 3.2)
assertion_results.evidence: affects-versions.md lines 9-12 show a 'Current vs Proposed' table: Current (PSIRT-assigned) = 'RHTPA 2.0.0', Proposed (lock file evidence) = 'RHTPA 2.2.0, RHTPA 2.2.1, RHTPA 2.2.2'. The diff is also shown in the comment on line 45: 'Corrected Affects Versions: [RHTPA 2.0.0] -> [RHTPA 2.2.0, RHTPA 2.2.1, RHTPA 2.2.2].' Rationale explains why RHTPA 2.0.0 is incorrect and which versions are affected based on lock file analysis.

assertion_results.text: Affects Versions correction references version names from the supportability matrix rather than hardcoded Jira version IDs (Important Rule 6)
assertion_results.evidence: affects-versions.md uses version names throughout: 'RHTPA 2.2.0', 'RHTPA 2.2.1', 'RHTPA 2.2.2'. The Jira update code block (lines 31-38) uses placeholder IDs like '<RHTPA-2.2.0-jira-id>' rather than hardcoded numeric IDs, and line 40 explicitly states: 'Version IDs would be discovered dynamically via getJiraIssueTypeMetaWithFields (not hardcoded).'

assertion_results.text: Remediation tasks include Repository, Target Branch, acceptance criteria, and dependency linkage between upstream and downstream tasks (Important Rule 9)
assertion_results.evidence: remediation.md Task 1 (upstream backport): Repository = 'backend' (line 23), Target Branch = 'release/0.4.z' (line 27), Acceptance Criteria checklist (lines 59-61), Dependencies (line 69). Task 2 (downstream propagation): Repository = 'rhtpa-release.0.4.z' (line 83), Target Branch = 'main' (line 87), Acceptance Criteria (lines 107-108), Dependencies showing upstream dependency (lines 115-116). Jira Linkage section (lines 122-131) shows Blocks dependency between upstream and downstream tasks.

assertion_results.text: For Cargo ecosystem, remediation creates two tasks: upstream backport task and downstream propagation subtask with Blocks dependency (Important Rule 8)
assertion_results.evidence: remediation.md creates Task 1 'Upstream Backport Task (2.2.x)' (line 15) and Task 2 'Downstream Propagation Subtask (2.2.x)' (line 73). The Blocks dependency is established in the Jira Linkage section line 127: 'jira.create_link(inwardIssue: <upstream-task-key>, outwardIssue: <downstream-task-key>, type: "Blocks")'. Line 7 confirms: 'Ecosystem: Cargo (source dependency) -- requires two tasks per affected stream: an upstream backport task and a downstream propagation subtask.'

assertion_results.text: The analysis presents triage recommendations as proposed actions — framing Affects Versions changes, label additions, and status transitions as proposals rather than executed mutations (Guardrails)
assertion_results.evidence: affects-versions.md uses 'Proposed' framing throughout: 'Current vs Proposed' table header (line 9), 'Proposed (lock file evidence)' (line 12), 'Proposed Jira Update' section heading (line 29) with a pseudocode block showing the API call rather than confirming execution. remediation.md presents Jira API calls in code blocks as planned actions (lines 122-131, 270-279) without execution confirmations or response data. All tasks are described with their specifications but no Jira issue keys are returned (placeholders like '<upstream-task-key>' are used), confirming no mutations were executed.

assertion_results.text: The data-extraction output describes Step 0.7 actions before the Step 1 data table: assigning the CVE issue to the current user and transitioning it to Assigned status (Step 0.7)
assertion_results.evidence: data-extraction.md begins with 'Step 0.7 -- Assign and Transition to Assigned' (line 1) before 'Step 1 -- Data Extraction' (line 33). Step 0.7 describes four actions: (1) retrieve current user's Jira account ID via jira.user_info(), (2) assign TC-8001 to the current user via jira.edit_issue, (3) discover the Assigned transition via jira.get_transitions, (4) transition to Assigned via jira.transition_issue. The section concludes on line 29: 'TC-8001 is now assigned to the current user and in Assigned status.'
